title: Local electronic structure and dynamics of hydrogen in $\text{CeO}_2$
authors: A. Koda, T. U. Ito, M. Hiraishi, H. Okabe, R. Kadono
abstract: The local electronic states of muon (Mu) as an isotope of hydrogen (H) in high-quality single-crystalline ceria (CeO$_2$) are investigated using muon spin rotation/relaxation ($μ\text{SR}$) and first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Upon positive muon implantation, both paramagnetic (Mu$^0$) and diamagnetic (Mu$^*$) states are observed below $\approx$10 K. Magnetic field dependence of the Mu$^0$ signals combined with DFT results provides evidence for the formation of a polaron state, consisting of Mu bonded to a ligand oxygen and a $4f$ electron localized on a nearby Ce site. The crystal-orientation dependence of the Mu$^0$ signal suggests strong anisotropy of the $4f$ electron due to the spin-orbit coupling with lifted degeneracy. Furthermore, the Mu$^0$ state exhibits transition to the Mu$^*$ state that corresponds to another Mu$^0$ state (exhibiting a diamagnetic response due to fast spin/charge fluctuations) at temperatures above $\approx$10 K, before disappearing above $\approx$30 K. These findings suggest rapid diffusive motion of the $4f$ electrons and/or Mu (as well as H) at higher temperatures.
summary: This work uses muon spin relaxation ($\mu	ext{SR}$) combined with DFT to characterize how hydrogen interacts electronically within ceria. It identifies two key electronic states—a polaron state and an atomic-like state—whose coexistence and transition are highly dependent on temperature. The findings illuminate the complex charge dynamics involving localized $4f$ electrons in correlated oxide materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the local electronic structure and dynamics of hydrogen (H) implanted into ceria ($	ext{CeO}_2$), aiming to understand its charge state and interaction with localized $4f$ electrons.
main_result: The muon spin relaxation data suggest the existence of two distinct states, a polaron-like $	ext{Mu}^0_{	ext{oxy}2}$ state and an atom-like $	ext{Mu}^*_{	ext{oct}}$ state, whose transition is temperature-dependent.
method: The research combines Muon Spin Rotation/Relaxation ($\mu	ext{SR}$) measurements with first-principles Density Functional Theory ($	ext{DFT}$) calculations to model the electronic states.
model_or_system: The system is interstitial hydrogen in single-crystalline ceria. The analysis models charge transfer between H and localized $4f$ electrons on Ce sites, leading to polaron formation or other defect configurations.
key_observables: Muon spin relaxation rate ($\lambda$), partial asymmetry ($A_p$), initial polarization ($P_z(0)$), and temperature dependence of these signals.
important_parameters: Low temperatures ($\approx 10 	ext{ K}$ to $30 	ext{ K}$), magnetic field strength, and the formation energy difference between proposed electronic states.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that $\mu	ext{SR}$ signals can be mapped onto specific electronic configurations predicted by DFT, although discrepancies suggest approximations like treating $4f$ electrons as free spins are insufficient.
figures_summary: Figures show $\mu	ext{SR}$ time spectra under varying magnetic fields and crystal orientations; temperature dependence plots illustrate the transition between paramagnetic ($\mu^0$) and diamagnetic ($\mu^*$) states, fitted using conversion models.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first presenting low-temperature $\mu	ext{SR}$ data showing two muon states. It then uses DFT to propose polaron/atomic structures. Subsequent analysis focuses on fitting the temperature and field dependence of the relaxation rates to confirm a dynamic transition between these proposed electronic states.
why_interesting: The interplay between localized $4f$ electrons (a hallmark of strongly correlated systems) and an interstitial impurity ($	ext{H}/\mu^+$) provides a direct experimental probe into charge localization, polaron formation, and defect physics in oxides.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a)–(d) Electronic structures of interstitial H in CeO2 pre- dicted by DFT calculations: H is bonded to O with an unpaired f electron located at (a), (b) Ce1 site, or (c) Ce2 site. The H-O-Ce1 an- gle in (a) is ≈180◦(oxy1). The O-H-O angle (connected by dashed lines) is (b) ≈168◦(oxy2) and (c) ≈175◦(oxy3). (d) Atom-like H is located at the Ce-cornered octahedral (oct) site. (e) Schematics of the electronic levels associated with H in the energy band diagram (after Ref.[9]), where the E(+/0) level for the oct conﬁguration is in- accessible due to the instability of the H+ state.

FIG. 2. µSR time spectra observed at 8.8 K under various external magnetic ﬁelds. The spectrum under TF (= 2 mT) represents sig- nals from the diamagnetic Mu∗state with the partial asymmetry A∗p and the non-relaxing diamagnetic Mu signal(s) with Ad (including background), whereas those under LF (= 0 to 300 mT) involve the signal from Mu0 state whose partial asymmetry Ap(B) varies with LF (where Ap0 is the maximum value for B →∞).

FIG. 3. Temperature dependence of (a) the partial asymmetry Ap0 (quasi-static Mu0), A∗p (Mu∗), and diamagnetic Mu, where the dot- dashed curve shows the behavior of Ap at LF = 20 mT. (b) The re- laxation rate of the A∗p component under TF = 2 mT (λ⊥) and LF = 20 mT (λ ∗) versus temperature. (See the main text for the dashed curves.)

FIG. 4. (a) LF dependence of the initial polarization Pz(0) observed for LF parallel with the crystal axis ⟨001⟩, ⟨110⟩, and ⟨111⟩(see text for the dashed curves). The calculated LF dependence of Pz(0) for the corresponding three directions assuming the local structures shown in Fig. 1(a)-(c) with a free 1µB spin placed at each Ce site. Inset: comparison of Pz(0) with B ∥⟨111⟩between oxy1 and oxy2 over a ﬁeld range of 4–40 mT around the inﬂection.

FIG. 5. LF dependence of (a) the initial asymmetry A(0) and (b) relaxation rate observed for LF parallel with the crystal axis ⟨001⟩. Dashed/dot-dashed curves are results of curve ﬁts assuming the con- version process from quasi-static Mu0 oxy2 to dynamical Mu∗oct states described by Eqs. (4) and (5) (see text for detail).
